topics.title: Introduction to C#
topics.definition: C# is a modern, object-oriented programming language developed by Microsoft. It runs on the .NET Framework or .NET Core platform. Visual Studio is the primary IDE for C# development. A C# program has a structure: using System; brings in the System namespace, namespace groups related code, class Program contains the entry point, and static void Main(string[] args) is where execution begins.

example.output: Hello, World!

topics.title: Basic Syntax
topics.definition:
Console.WriteLine(): prints text and moves to the next line.
Console.Write(): prints text without a newline.
Console.ReadLine(): reads a full line from the user.
Comments: // for single-line, /* ... */ for multi-line.
Variables: named storage locations with a type (int, double, float, char, string, bool).
Data Types: int (whole numbers), double/float (decimals), char (single character), string (text), bool (true/false).
Type Casting: convert from one type to another (implicit or explicit).
var keyword: compiler infers type from the assigned value.
const keyword: constant value that cannot be changed after initialization.

example.output:
=== Basic IO ===
Enter your name: Ali
Enter your age: 22
Hello Ali, you are 22 years old.
C# syntax demo

topics.title: Operators
topics.definition:
Arithmetic Operators (+, -, *, /, %): perform basic math (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, remainder).
Assignment Operators (=, +=, -=, *=, /=, %=): assign or update the value of a variable.
Comparison Operators (==, !=, >, <, >=, <=): compare two values and return a bool.
Logical Operators (&&, ||, !): combine or negate boolean expressions.
Increment / Decrement (++, --): increase or decrease a numeric value by 1.
Ternary Operator (condition ? expr1 : expr2): short form of if-else that returns one of two values based on a condition.

example.output:
Arithmetic:
x + y = 13
x - y = 7
x * y = 30
x / y = 3
x % y = 1

Comparison & Logical:
x == y ? False
x > 0 && y > 0 ? True

Ternary:
x is greater

topics.title: Conditional Statements
topics.definition:
if: run a block when condition is true.
if-else: run one block when true, another when false.
else if: chain multiple conditions in order.
Nested if: place if statement inside another if to check deeper logic.
switch: select one case based on a value; cleaner alternative to many else if for fixed options.

example.output:
Grade: B
Today is: Wednesday

topics.title: Loops
topics.definition:
for: best when you know the exact number of iterations (has initializer, condition, increment).
while: repeats as long as the condition is true (may run zero times).
do-while: runs the body at least once, then checks the condition.
foreach: iterates over all elements in a collection or array.
break: exits the nearest loop immediately.
continue: skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next.

example.output:
for loop (1 to 3):
1
2
3

while loop (countdown 3 to 1):
3
2
1

foreach over array:
C#
Python

topics.title: Methods (Functions)
topics.definition: Methods have syntax: return type, name, parameters. void methods return nothing. Parameters pass data in. Default parameters have default values. Method overloading: same name, different parameters. ref passes by reference, out returns value through parameter.

example.output: Sum: 8

topics.title: Arrays
topics.definition: Single dimensional array: int[] arr. Multi-dimensional: int[,] matrix. Jagged array: int[][] - array of arrays. Array properties: Length gives size. Arrays are zero-indexed.

topics.title: Strings
topics.definition: String methods: ToUpper(), ToLower() for case. Trim() removes whitespace. Substring() extracts part. Replace() substitutes text. String interpolation $"" embeds variables. StringBuilder is for efficient string building.

example.output:
HELLO WORLD
 Hello
Name: Ali

topics.title: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
topics.definition: Class is blueprint, Object is instance. Properties encapsulate data with get/set. Constructor initializes object. Destructor cleans up. Encapsulation hides internal state. Abstraction shows only essential. Inheritance: derived class gets base members. Polymorphism: same interface, different behavior. virtual/override for method overriding. sealed prevents inheritance. this refers to current instance.

example.output: Ali

topics.title: Collections
topics.definition: List<T> - dynamic list. Dictionary<TKey,TValue> - key-value pairs. Stack - LIFO. Queue - FIFO. HashSet - unique elements. Collections are in System.Collections.Generic.

topics.title: Exception Handling
topics.definition: try block contains risky code. catch handles exceptions. finally always runs. Custom exception: create class inheriting Exception. throw raises exception.

example.output:
5
Done

topics.title: Advanced Topics
topics.definition: Generics: type-safe reusable code with <T>. Interfaces define contract without implementation. Abstract class: base class with abstract members. Dependency Injection: inject dependencies for loose coupling. Entity Framework: ORM for database. ASP.NET MVC: web framework. Web API: RESTful APIs.

example.output: Hello!

topics.title: Introduction to .NET
topics.definition: .NET is a cross-platform framework by Microsoft. .NET Core (now .NET 5+) is modern and cross-platform. MVC architecture separates Model, View, Controller. Project structure: Program.cs is entry point, appsettings.json holds configuration.

example.output: ASP.NET Core MVC app starts

topics.title: MVC Basics
topics.definition: Models represent data and logic. Views display UI (Razor). Controllers handle requests and return Views. Routing maps URLs to controller actions. Layout Page _Layout.cshtml provides shared structure. Razor syntax mixes C# with HTML using @.

example.output: Returns Index view with ViewBag

topics.title: Database Setup (Entity Framework Core)
topics.definition: Install EF Core NuGet packages. Create Model classes. Create DbContext. Set connection string in appsettings.json. Add-Migration creates migration. Update-Database applies to DB.

example.output: User model and DbContext

topics.title: Login & Signup (Without Identity)
topics.definition: User Model: Id, Name, Email, Password, Role. Signup: Register view, HTTP GET/POST, save user, check duplicate email, store password (hashed preferred). Login: Login view, check email/password in DB, redirect to Dashboard if valid, show error if invalid. Session: HttpContext.Session.SetString/GetString. Logout clears session. Check session in Controller, redirect to Login if null.

example.output: Session-based login

topics.title: CRUD After Login
topics.definition: Only logged-in users can access. Create: add new records. Read: list and details. Update: edit existing. Delete: remove records. Protect controllers with session check before each action.

example.output: Protected Create action

topics.title: Validation
topics.definition: Required fields: [Required]. Email format: [EmailAddress]. Password confirmation: compare two fields. Use ModelState.IsValid in controller. Display errors with asp-validation-for.

example.output: Model with validation

topics.title: Extra Security
topics.definition: Password Hashing: use BCrypt or similar, never store plain text. Try-Catch: wrap DB/risky operations. ModelState.IsValid: always validate before processing. Validate input, use parameterized queries.

example.output: User saved with hashed password

topics.title: Introduction to HTML
topics.definition: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages. It uses tags to describe the structure and content of a page. Every HTML document starts with a <!DOCTYPE html> declaration, followed by <html>, <head>, and <body> elements.
topics.syntax:
Main Heading

Paragraph text.

example.code:
Welcome to HTML

This is a simple web page.
example.output: A web page with a browser title "My First Page", an H1 heading "Welcome to HTML" and a paragraph "This is a simple web page."

topics.title: Elements & Attributes
topics.definition:
HTML elements are building blocks defined by tags like

,

,

. Elements can have attributes that provide extra information, such as id, class, href, src, alt. Attributes are written inside the start tag as name="value".
topics.syntax: Content Visit

example.code:
Hello

This paragraph has a class attribute.

Open Example
example.output: A heading "Hello", a styled paragraph, and a link that opens example.com in a new tab.

topics.title: Lists & Links
topics.definition: HTML supports ordered lists (<ol>) and unordered lists (<ul>) containing list items (<li>). Links are created with the <a> tag and the href attribute. You can create navigation menus with lists and links.
topics.syntax:
• Item 1
  • Item 2
  1. First
  2. Second
Go to page

example.code:
• Home
  • About
  • Contact
example.output: A simple navigation menu with three links: Home, About, Contact.

topics.title: Forms & Inputs
topics.definition: Forms collect user input and submit it to a server. Use the <form> element with action and method attributes. Inside, use input elements (text, email, password), <textarea>, <select>, and <button>.
topics.syntax:
<form action="/submit" method="post">
  <label>Name:</label>
  <input type="text" name="name">
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>

example.code:
<form>
  <label for="email">Email:</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>

  <label for="msg">Message:</label>
  <textarea id="msg" name="message"></textarea>

  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
example.output: A contact form with email and message fields and a submit button.

topics.title: Semantic HTML
topics.definition: Semantic elements describe their meaning to both the browser and the developer. Examples: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <footer>. They improve accessibility and SEO.
topics.syntax:
<header>Site header</header>
<nav>Navigation links</nav>
<main>
  <section>
    <article>Article content</article>
  </section>
</main>
<footer>Footer info</footer>

example.code:
My Blog

Home Posts

First Post

This is my first article.

© 2026 My Blog
example.output: A basic blog layout using semantic tags for header, navigation, main content, and footer.

topics.title: Introduction to CSS
topics.definition: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the presentation of HTML elements: colors, fonts, layout. CSS rules consist of a selector and a declaration block of property-value pairs.

example.code:
Styled Heading

This text uses the global font and dark background.
example.output: A page with a dark background, light text, and a cyan-colored H1 heading.

topics.title: Selectors & Colors
topics.definition: CSS selectors target elements: type selectors (p), class selectors (.btn), id selectors (#header), and attribute selectors. Colors can be defined with names, hex, rgb(), or hsl().

example.code:
Welcome

This is a normal paragraph.

This paragraph is highlighted.
example.output: Heading "Welcome" in green, normal gray paragraph, and a yellow highlighted paragraph.

topics.title: Box Model
topics.definition: Every element is a box with content, padding, border, and margin. The box model affects element size and spacing. The box-sizing property controls whether width includes padding and border.

example.code:
Card Title

This card demonstrates padding, margin, and border.
example.output: A dark card with inner spacing (padding), outer space (margin), border, and rounded corners.

topics.title: Flexbox Layout
topics.definition: Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout system for aligning items in a row or column. Use display: flex on a container, then properties like justify-content, align-items, and gap.

example.code:
Box 1
Box 2
Box 3
example.output: Three equal-width boxes in a row with space between them.

topics.title: Typography
topics.definition: Typography in CSS includes font-family, font-size, font-weight, line-height, and text alignment. Use web-safe fonts or custom fonts via @font-face or Google Fonts.

example.code:
Heading

This is smaller helper text.
example.output: A large bold heading with smaller, muted helper text underneath.

topics.title: Introduction to JavaScript
topics.definition: JavaScript is a high-level, interpreted programming language that runs in the browser and on servers (Node.js). It adds interactivity to web pages: dynamic content, forms, events, and more.
topics.syntax:
<script>
  console.log('Hello, JavaScript');
</script>

example.code: JS Demo
example.output: A page that shows an alert "Welcome to JavaScript" and logs "Hello from JS" in the browser console.

topics.title: Variables & Data Types
topics.definition: Use let and const to declare variables. JavaScript has primitive types: number, string, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, and bigint. Variables are dynamically typed.
topics.syntax:
let age = 25;        // number
const name = 'Ali';  // string
let isActive = true; // boolean

example.code:
<script>
  let age = 25;
  const name = 'Ali';
  let isStudent = true;

  console.log('Name:', name);
  console.log('Age:', age);
  console.log('Student:', isStudent);
</script>
example.output: Console logs: Name: Ali, Age: 25, Student: true

topics.title: Functions
topics.definition: Functions group reusable code. You can declare them with function keyword or as arrow functions. Functions can take parameters and return values.

example.code:
<script>
  function greet(name) {
    return `Hello, ${name}`;
  }

  const result = greet('Ali');
  console.log(result);
</script>
example.output: Console logs: Hello, Ali

topics.title: DOM Manipulation
topics.definition: The DOM (Document Object Model) represents the page as a tree of nodes. JavaScript can select elements and change their content, attributes, and styles using methods like getElementById and querySelector.
topics.syntax:
const title = document.getElementById('title');
title.textContent = 'New Title';

example.code: Old Title
example.output: Clicking the button changes the H1 text to "Title changed by JS".

topics.title: Events
topics.definition: Events are actions that happen in the browser: clicks, key presses, mouse moves, form submissions. Use addEventListener to listen and respond.

example.code:
<button id="counter-btn">Clicked 0 times</button>

<script>
  const btn = document.getElementById('counter-btn');
  let count = 0;
  btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
    count++;
    btn.textContent = `Clicked ${count} times`;
  });
</script>
example.output: Each click on the button updates the text: "Clicked 1 times", "Clicked 2 times", etc.
